

-- 1 of 15 --



-- 2 of 15 --


www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
The Five Internet Marketing Models …
The 5 Internet Marketing Models
I’ve Used to Make Well Over
$100 Million for My Clients
Okay, so you’ve done your due diligence. You’ve found a client that’s both trustworthy and ready
to grow. He’s positioned very uniquely in his market.
And you’ve made sure that you’re not only dealing with a one-of-a-kind product, but you’ve also taken
the steps to make the most irresistible possible offer.
NOW WHAT?
Simple: You must address the question of HOW you’re going to market the product – in other words,
the “marketing model” you’re going to use.
What’s that you ask? I’ll tell you: Your marketing model describes the strategy – the step-by-step
process – you’re going to use to:
1. Find your best prospects …
2. Turn those prospects into customers in the most cost-effective way possible …
3. And ultimately, to cause your customers to:
a. Buy from you more often …
b. Spend more with you on each purchase, and …
c. Keep buying from you longer; hopefully, forever.
Get that right and you’re on your way. But if you screw it up, even the best product ever invented and
most brilliant sales copy ever written won’t save your sorry butt.
How NOT to Do It
Over the years, I invited various Total Package customers to ask me anything they liked – and it’s
always a blast.
One time I remember very vividly how a young copywriter had just invested several weeks of his life
creating a one-shot direct mail promotion to convince personal trainers to buy a $200,000 franchise in
his client’s fitness business.
The client mailed about 400 of his sales letters, then sat back, expecting a tsunami of samoleans to
come crashing in.
But instead, they received:

-- 3 of 15 --

4
The Makepeace Business System
The Five Internet Marketing Models …
* Fewer inquiries than you have toes on your left foot …
* Zero sales.
Now, I never saw my friend’s copy. I don’t have to. Because even the best sales copy ever written in
the history of direct response would have failed miserably in this case.
Fact is, there’s so much wrong with this marketing model, I hardly know where to begin.
For one thing, 400 sales letters to sell a $200,000 product? Let’s think about that for a moment …
A single order would give you a .25% (one-quarter of one percent) response rate. Now, that’s a
reasonable response to expect if you’re offering a $200 product and IF your prospects are crazy
qualified – but not even conceivable with a hard offer and at a price that’s one thousand times higher.
But I suspect that my friend’s prospects were nowhere near qualified to spend even two dollars with
him – let alone two hundred thousand.
See, I’ve had a few personal trainers over the years. And every one of them had one thing in
common.
Care to guess what it was?
Bingo: Every one of them was flat broke. None of them could have shelled out $200,000 for a fitness
franchise if you’d held a gun to their heads.
In fact, I’d be willing to bet most of my friend’s prospects have second and even third jobs just to
make ends meet. Heck: I met my favorite trainer the night she served me a margarita at my favorite
tiki bar.
(It was an interesting relationship: Every weekend at the bar, she’d keep my glass full of high-calorie
booze. Then, Monday through Friday, she’d come to the house and beat me mercilessly; cheerfully
exacting sadistic revenge for every drink she’d served me over the weekend.)
But even if every one of the 400 folks who got my friend’s sales letter had two-hundred-thousand
buckaroonis burning a hole in their pockets, my Total Package reader was still screwed majorly
before he began writing his sales letter. He was screwed the minute he accepted the client.
Because frankly, the franchise owner was an idiot.
See, my friend tried to tell him that you don’t sell $200,000 products in a single sales letter. Your
prospect doesn’t know you from Adam – and you expect him to read a few pages of sales copy, then
cut you a check for one-fifth of a million smackers?
Sorry – but no matter how hypnotic your sales copy is … no matter how many happy thoughts the
client thinks … I’ve got news for you: The franchise owner’s got a monumental case of cranium ca-ca
if he thinks the world really works that way.
Because all things being equal …
√ As your price rises, so does you prospects’ skepticism.
√ Most folks will spend $9.99, $19, even $29 with a total stranger without a second

-- 4 of 15 --

5
www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
The Five Internet Marketing Models …
thought.
√ If they’re well qualified for your offer, they’ll spend $49, $79, $99, even $199 so long
as they see a few reasonable proof elements and a convincing guarantee.
√ But if you want a prospect to cut you a check for much more than that, you’ll need to
do more than just answer every objection and erase his skepticism.
You’ll need to establish a trusting relationship with him.
The Better Way to Make a $200,000 Sale …
If your price is $200,000, you’re going to have to work get it. You’ll have to mount a multi-step
marketing campaign that uses the mail, the telephone and maybe even a one-on-one meeting to
move the prospect, one careful step at a time, from where he is now to where you want him to be.
Or, it could be an intense online blitz – a blizzard of e-mails and banner ads, a PPC campaign and
maybe even offline media like TV, radio and direct mail – to drive prospects to a search-engine-
optimized landing page that produces a lead, then follows with maybe a direct mail kit, one or more
phone conversations and perhaps even the face-to-face stuff.
So how would I have done it?
Well, I would have begun by asking myself, “Who’s most likely to buy a $200,000 franchise?”
And I’d probably have answered myself, “I’m not certain – but it sure as heck ain’t poverty-stricken
physical trainers!”
Instead, I would have begun by beating the bushes for mailing lists, magazines and websites that
deliver the eyeballs of people who have proven that they want to go into business for themselves.
I would have tested, for example, lists of subscribers to magazines that target folks who want to start
their own businesses – and I would have run lead-producing ads in those publications as well. Biz-
op book and product buyers would have been worth a direct mail test, too – and especially folks who
have inquired about or bought other franchises.
If I wanted to spread a wider net, I would have done it on the cheap: With lead-producing inserts in
card decks, other people’s product shipments and other media that let me solicit inquiries for $5 or
$10 per thousand eyeballs. But never, never, never in a direct mail package that costs me fifty to one
hundred times more to mail!
In my first communication with my leads and prospective buyers, I’d set out to seduce them with vivid
mental pictures of life as a business owner. I’d romance the freedom, the prestige, and the money.
I’d demonstrate that the business I’m selling is easy, fun and a great way to meet interesting people
and profit like crazy.
And I’d load my promotion with case histories and testimonials from people just like him who have
used this franchise to become wildly successful.
I wouldn’t even come close to naming my price in my first contact – but I would trivialize it by
demonstrating how buying this franchise is like buying thousand-dollar bills for a penny apiece. Or

-- 5 of 15 --

6
The Makepeace Business System
The Five Internet Marketing Models …
better yet, that our easy financing plan will put him in business cheap and have him earning big profits
fast.
Then, I’d add an urgency motivator – like mentioning that only one franchise will be granted in his city,
on a first-come, first-served basis. And I’d ask him to call or maybe write his phone number and e-mail
addy on a reply card and return it to me NOW.
The ones that call, I’d qualify on the phone. The ones that don’t, would get a second “Why haven’t I
heard from you?” letter – or if the list is responding well, an outbound call from a franchise rep.
That’s how I’d do it – and that’s how my Total Package friend wanted to do it, too.
“But no-o-o-o,” says the client. “I don’t want a multi-step marketing model. I want you to sell a
$200,000 franchise to people who probably can’t afford one in a single sales letter.”
Sure, pal – I’ll get right on it. Soon as I part the Red Sea, turn a few cases of Dasani into Cristal and
raise a coupla dudes from the dead.
So that’s how I might do a super-high-ticket promotion. But what about others – like the projects we
work on every day here at ResponseInk – and the projects you’re most likely to be tackling?
Entire books have been written about this; there’s no way to cover it all in a short article like this one,
so I’m not about to try.
I will, however, give you a great starting point for getting a great marketing model …
Steal it.
Find a company that’s marketing a product similar to yours … that sells at a price-point similar to
yours … to prospects like yours … and shamelessly duplicate THEIR marketing model.
I mean – why re-invent the wheel? There are hundreds, maybe thousands of successful companies
in every niche you can name. The most successful ones have spent years and millions of dollars in
slow, painful trial-and-error testing to find the optimal prospect/media/messaging/offer mix.
The conclusions they’ve drawn from all that priceless marketing data is easy to see; you just have to
watch what they’re doing.
When I begin work with a new client in a new niche, the first thing I want to know is, “What’s the name
of the five most successful companies in this niche?”
Once I have those names, the questions come fast and furious: “What kinds of prospects are they
targeting? What media are they using? What kind of offers are they making? What price-points am I
seeing most often? What kinds of sales copy and formats are they using to attract new customers?”
I grab the appropriate SRDS (usually the mail list edition) and look up my target companies for clues
on how fast they’re growing (by studying the number of hotline names they have available) … how
they generate their customers … what their price-points are, and more.
I check Target Marketing’s Who’s Mailing What to see if they have any samples of my targets’
promotions on file.

-- 6 of 15 --

7
www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
The Five Internet Marketing Models …
I buy something from the companies I’m studying, suspecting that they’ll also be sending their
new customer acquisition promos to their customer file. And I buy something from their largest
competitors, hoping my target companies will rent those lists and I’ll be able to see the promotions
they use to attract new customers.
I visit the popular websites and subscribe to the e-zines in their market niche to see if they’re placing
banners on those sites or sponsoring those e-zines. I click every link that I suspect may lead me to
that company’s squeeze pages, landing pages or websites. If they have an e-zine, I subscribe. If their
website invites me to register, I register.
In short, I do everything I can to make sure the company has my phone number … my street address
… and my e-mail address so I can see as much of what they’re doing as possible.
Then, once I have a clear picture of how my target company attracts its new customers, I simply
“borrow” (OK – “steal”) their marketing model from them.
At this stage, I don’t want to innovate. I just want to help my client do things as well as the largest,
most successful company in his niche does. Once we’re doing that, it’s time to test new stuff. But for
now, being as good as the best is good enough.
5 Internet Marketing Models
Every Good Partner Should Know About
I know I’ve been a little vague up to this point, but I wanted to give you a glimpse of the thinking
process that goes into selecting the right marketing model.
Once you’re clear on this – and you’ve begun to incorporate it into your own thought process – you’re
ready to learn about the specific marketing models that are used most often online.
Also, remember that all of the following models are going to use some form of “list marketing” (though
the emphasis will certainly vary) – because list marketing is always a cornerstone of the direct
response process.
Here they are …
The E-zine Model
The e-zine model is something I KNOW you’re familiar with – because it’s what we use here at The
Total Package! The model works like this …
1. Develop a unique position and viewpoint in your intended niche market (it could be
gardening, dating, personal finance, Internet marketing … the possibilities are endless).
The most important thing is that you’ve got something powerful to say – something that prospects
are eager to hear and know about – and that they’re willing to believe you’re both unique and worth
listening to.
2. Once you have this, you build an e-zine around the subject your prospects are interested in.
Treat it exactly as you would a paid product – make sure that it delivers information that will help them
solve pressing problems …

-- 7 of 15 --

8
The Makepeace Business System
The Five Internet Marketing Models …
… And fulfill their innermost desires. Make sure that the advice and information you provide is well-
organized, clear, and easy-to-act-upon.
Sound familiar? When we first launched The Total Package – I wrote a series of compelling articles
and delivered exceptional content that had never quite been available in this market. It reinforced the
uniqueness and expertise I had as a marketer and teacher – and created a passionate following of
readers who firmly believed my team and I were worth listening to.
3. Give it away for FREE. In this model, the purpose of your e-zine is multi-faceted …
a) You want to earn your prospect’s trust by giving him valuable information …
b) You want to prove that you’re a bonafide expert – the “go to guy” in your niche – and
that he should listen to you …
c) You want to give him a justifiable, self-centered reason for handing over his name
and e-mail so that you can contact him on a regular basis … and …
d) You want to have a reason for making contact that’s purely for HIS sake – and not
just to sell him something …
e) You want to turn these regular contacts into selling opportunities that you can use to
grow your business …
Makes sense, right? Just like we did with The Total Package – your e-zine becomes the tool you use
to build a relationship with potential prospects – and then improve their lives as you offer them all the
wonderful products you’ve created!
4. Once you’ve established your e-zine, use every contact as an opportunity to make a sale.
To do this, you can …
Include ads for the products you’ve created inside your e-zine (the more people who
subscribe, the better your ads, and the more products you have – the more money you’ll
make) …
You can send marketing e-mails totally separate from your e-zine that drive prospects
directly to your sales letters …
You can run product launches and marketing campaigns to your list (more on product
launches in just a second).
The list goes on.
The key things to remember about the e-zine model are:
- It’s a commitment (so make sure you can deliver the content as regularly as you
promise) …
- You’ve got to have plenty of free content to give away (if you’ve only got enough free
info for a few articles, then the model isn’t going to work. You’ll run out of things to say
and you’ll either be forced to stop, or you’ll bore your prospects into unsubscribing) …

-- 8 of 15 --


www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
The Five Internet Marketing Models …
- You need multiple products to sell … good e-mail copy … and good sales letters – IF
you want to make it profitable …
- Generally the more contacts you make – the more sales you make. Remember, every
time your prospect reads something from you – there’s an opportunity for him to buy
from you. So publishing your e-zine or e-letter once a week, multiple times a week – or
even daily – will help you to grow more aggressively …
As a prospective partner, you probably won’t be forced to handle all the minute details of executing an
e-zine on a daily, weekly or monthly basis.
But you WILL need to understand the model … and be able to advise the client on his or her
positioning, plus the kind of content that should be delivered. The key is to give prospects enough
valuable content in your letter that they love you and are bonded to you, while at the same time
wetting their appetite for products that go deeper and give him an opportunity to achieve greater
mastery.
The E-Commerce Model
This model is very different than the e-zine model, but there can be some crossover.
For example, here at The Total Package – we do things to drive organic search traffic … and we also
have an online store that sells our products.
This is the essence of the e-commerce model:
1) Have a big online store that sells lots of desirable products. (The copy used to sell the
products can be short and catalogue-like – think Best Buy or Macy’s – or you can use full blown sales
letters.)
2) Drive lots of targeted traffic to your site – using both SEO (search engine optimization) and paid
media (banner ads, Google Adwords, etc.)
The companies that succeed with e-commerce do both of these things exceptionally well.
Of course, there are many things you can do to enhance this process.
You can also …
Provide great articles on your site that help drive organic traffic (just like we do with The Total
Package) …
Make the buying process more interactive by offering prospects the ability to read/provide product
reviews (think Amazon) …
You can make your site easy to navigate, so that prospects stick around longer and can quickly get to
the products they’re considering buying …
PLUS, you easily incorporate the e-zine model into your business. It’s just a matter of enticing the
prospects who come to your site to opt-in and begin receiving your e-mail communications and
marketing …

-- 9 of 15 --

10
The Makepeace Business System
The Five Internet Marketing Models …
Again, success within this model usually depends on you being able to offer a good volume of unique,
desirable products – then driving massive amounts of targeted traffic to the website.
As a partner, you won’t be expected to know everything about driving traffic – leave that up to experts
in that arena – but you really do need to know how that model works so that you can advise your
clients appropriately.
The Product Launch Model
If you’re on any of the lists of popular Internet marketing gurus –guys like Rich Schefren to Frank
Kern to Jeff Walker and others – then you undoubtedly have experienced the product launch model
before.
Essentially, this process is very low-involvement for much of the year – and then gets very intense
when a new product is being released.
I’ll give you an example …
Jeff Walker is the creator of an excellent product called Product Launch Formula. It discusses the
sequence and methodology for launching new products on the Web, using affiliates.
Here’s how it works:
The guru or expert comes up with a new product that’s desired by prospects in a specific niche (a
niche where there are lots of potential affiliates) …
The guru then goes to affiliates and says something to the effect of, “Hey I’ve got this amazing new
product … the marketing behind it is going to be amazing … the price-point is high (often between
$500-$3000) … and if you send out my marketing e-mails, I’ll give you 50% of the sale for anyone
that buys from your list!”
For most people with a list, this is an irresistible proposition – very close to free money. All they
have to do is send out their e-mails and then 50% of whatever they sell to people on their list is their
commission.
Once affiliates are on board, a sequence of marketing messages and communication takes place
– often a lot like this …
Stage One: Create Some Very Valuable Free Content – content closely related to the product
you’re going to sell – that you will use to build your list. The content can be videos, special reports,
interviews – and anything in between.
And the purpose of it is several-fold. It should:
a) Shock your market – and make you the center of attention …
b) It should entice your prospects into joining your e-mail list – so that you can market to
them later on …
c) And perhaps most importantly, it should psychologically predispose them to buy your
product once you finally release it …
Let me give you an example … I’m good friends with Rich Schefren, and he’s one of the most

-- 10 of 15 --

11
www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
The Five Internet Marketing Models …
legitimate, most successful gurus on the Web.
When Rich first came onto the Internet marketing scene, he discovered that both he and many of the
other successful business owners were working way too hard for the money they were making. Even
worse, they were miserable – they hated their business … it consumed their lives … and they had no
time for their families or the things they loved.
So Rich started working with many of the top marketers, helping them step back from their
businesses and develop systems that would allow them to accomplish much more – using less of
their own time and personal involvement.
After some time teaching this and creating some really amazing proof elements and success stories,
Rich created a scalable online coaching program that would help ANY business owner on the Web
get more done and build a “real” business – one that worked and made money even when he wasn’t
doing anything.
And when it came time to launch this new program, Rich wrote a controversial report titled The
Internet Business Manifesto – and then got a massive number of affiliates to promote it for him.
This report talked about the silent epidemic of Internet entrepreneurs working themselves to the bone
– often with nothing to show for it – and how a new way of doing things was at hand … a way for
them to work less, make more money – even when they were away from their business – and begin
living the lifestyle of their dreams.
The report quickly became the talk of the Internet marketing world – and spread across the Internet
like wildfire. Which leads us to the next stage …
Stage Two: Use Your Content to Build a Huge List. Whether you’re giving away videos, reports
or anything else at the beginning of your launch – your goal is to build a massive list of qualified
prospects.
So you must drive your prospects to a squeeze page (a “name capture” page that bribes the
prospect, offering him free stuff in exchange for his name and e-mail) that does a good job of
convincing him to join your list and go through your content.
In Rich’s cases, he used a squeeze page in which he promised to reveal the shocking truth being
exposed in the Manifesto … the benefits of reading it … the consequences of NOT reading it … and
so on.
This squeeze page is crucial because all of your affiliates will be sending e-mails for you that drive
prospects to your squeeze page (in Rich’s example, the affiliates would sing the praises of the
manifesto and then provided the link to the page where they could go and download it for free).
If your squeeze page sucks – or the content you’re promising is not attractive – then you won’t build a
big list. Which means you won’t sell as much as you could.
Stage Three: Begin Marketing to Your List. Once your affiliates have mailed and your list is in
place, you begin communicating with your list via e-mail. Your e-mails will begin by talking about the
problem and how the solution is at hand … and about how you’re going to be releasing a product that
will transform the prospect’s life.

-- 11 of 15 --

12
The Makepeace Business System
The Five Internet Marketing Models …
Once he knows this, you can create anticipation by emphasizing benefits he wants (Rich uses stories
of entrepreneurs making lots more money while enjoying much greater quality of life) … and then by
proving those benefits.
This period of sending out e-mails or driving prospects to your blog will be intensified as you get
closer to the release of the product. Often, you’ll also limit the quantities being sold and show the
prospect that a large numbers of people are looking to take the available products or slots that you’ll
have on release day.
Stage Four: Open the Gates. When people on the list are excited and eager to buy your product,
you then allow them to start buying. This happens by using e-mails that drive the prospect to your
sales letter for the product.
If you’ve limited the number you’re going to sell, you stop selling when you’ve reached your limit. If
you’ve created a deadline, you stop selling when you’ve told the prospects the deadline is up. Be sure
to keep your word.
And that’s pretty much it! Of course, executing this takes a lot of strategizing, work, planning and
execution. But the results can be VERY explosive. You can literally make millions of dollars in days.
Here are some things to remember and consider …
You can do a product launch within just about any of the other models – including e-zine
or e-commerce model (we have at The Total Package) …
It’s a fantastic way to build a list that you can then stay in touch with and sell affiliate
products to (more on this in a second) …
Many of the gurus who do big product launches don’t really sell their products much
throughout the year – they just focus their sales aggressively into the period where
they’re officially “launching” …
The product launch model – especially when you don’t sell other products year-round
– is pretty low maintenance, but will rarely result in a huge seven, eight or nine-figure
business …
Even if you don’t want to commit to a full-blown e-zine or newsletter, it’s still a good idea to maintain
positive contact with your list – so that they’re more bonded to you and thus more responsive when it
comes time to sell them stuff …
For added insights be sure to check out other products: My Online Profit Multiplier (OPM) or Jeff
Walker’s Product Launch Formula or Frank Kern’s Mass Control. All will give you the added insights
and secrets it takes to fully orchestrate successful launches.
The Affiliate Model
This model is for when you don’t really have products of your own. Early to Rise – the massively
successful Agora company – essentially started out as a blend between an e-letter and an affiliate
marketing engine.
You see, Early to Rise began as an amazing free e-letter that advised readers on the issues of health
and wealth. But they didn’t have any products.

-- 12 of 15 --

1
www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
The Five Internet Marketing Models …
So what they did was promote affiliate products within their newsletter.
And it worked amazing well! They grew like crazy – to the point where they were able to begin
creating their own products and services to market to their list.
The cool thing is you can do affiliate marketing ANYTIME you have traffic or a list.
For example, as you saw with the product launch model – there are times when you build up a big list
and then have no new products (of your own) to sell them.
But what you can do is sell affiliate products to your list! And this can be very lucrative, especially
considering that it costs you so little time. In fact, I know some marketers who make over $1 million a
year selling affiliate products to their list (remember, that’s not including sales and profits they make
selling their OWN products to their list!)
With affiliate marketing, many of the same rules and principles apply …
You want to create trust and a sense of bonding (your prospect should get to know you,
trust you and believe in your authority in whatever niche you’re in) …
You want to leverage this trust (when your prospect trusts what you’re saying and
believes in your authority, he will be more apt to buy affiliate products you recommend)
…
You want to give him value that has nothing to do with selling (if you constantly just
pound him with affiliate promotions, you’ll ruin the relationship and he’ll unsubscribe or
stop opening your e-mails) …
You want to make sure the affiliate products are trustworthy, in alignment with what you
teach and are things your prospect is interested in!
Remember, you’re vouching for the quality of the products you’re promoting – and your
prospect trusts you. Don’t injure the trust by recommending something that isn’t good.
Also, be sure it’s stuff you know he wants or is interested in – so it doesn’t appear
random. And make sure the products don’t challenge or erode the message that you’re
sending him.
If you can do all those things, the affiliate marketing model can be an amazing business or its own
profit center in your existing business.
The Blog Model
Finally, the blog model is something else to consider. It can function as its own business or as a part
of you overall business.
The blog model works very much like an e-zine or e-letter, with the main difference being the delivery
of the content and the regularity. Blogs can be much more random and unpredictable than the former.
The content quality and style is going to be very much like your e-zine, although readers will be more
forgiving for random, more emotional outbursts of writing on a blog than they will be on an e-letter.

-- 13 of 15 --

14
The Makepeace Business System
The Five Internet Marketing Models …
Blog marketing is based on driving traffic via the quality of your content – and by bonding with
prospects based on the quality of that content.
When large numbers of prospects are attracted to your blog, you then have ads for your products and
services on the blog.
Of course, there’s a science of getting your blog to be syndicated by other sites – and getting people
to agree to receive notifications when you make new posts.
But that’s not really what you’ll be responsible for during the process. Your job will usually just be to
help brainstorm the content delivered on the blog … and help create the ads that drive your prospects
to sales letters for the products you’re offering.
A very good example of how this works well in our niche can be seen at www.copyblogger.com
– Brian Clark is someone I’ve interviewed before and have a great deal of respect for.
In the End, It’s All
About Mixing and Matching!
As I wind down this report, I want to make it clear that you can use pieces of ALL the online marketing
models I’ve just described. It all depends on your clients business … personnel … available time …
budget … and so on.
For example, we at The Total Package use everything I’ve listed. We started with a free e-zine
that established a high level of trust and authority with our readers … we have a online store (e-
commerce) where people can go to buy our products … we use blogs and SEO to drive organic
traffic to our site … we’ve done big product launches before … and we’re starting to do some affiliate
marketing for complimentary products we believe in.
So keep this in mind as you go through your “Overnight Marketing Checklist” on the next page. It will
allow you to truly assist your client in developing the marketing strategies and steps he needs to grow.

-- 14 of 15 --



-- 15 of 15 --
